museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: October 2002: Vice City and Super Mario Sunshine crowd the autumn shelf.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for October 2002. Regional release dates are deliberately conservative and should be checked before verification.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Super Mario Sunshine
items.body: Mario's holiday island sells GameCube colour, water and oddness.
items.sourceNote: European timing is autumn 2002; Japan/US dates precede it.

items.title: TimeSplitters 2
items.body: Free Radical makes the split-screen shooter feel British, fast and generous.
items.sourceNote: Autumn 2002 timing varies by platform and region.

items.title: Grand Theft Auto: Vice City
items.body: Rockstar turns a boxed game into a radio station, wardrobe and schoolyard quote machine.
items.sourceNote: Vice City sources list late October 2002 in the US and autumn 2002 elsewhere.

items.title: Medal of Honor: Allied Assault
items.body: PC war shooters become cinematic, scripted and increasingly mainstream.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed as a January 2002 PC release.

items.title: Xbox European launch line-up
items.body: Halo reaches UK shelves as the new console's strongest argument.
items.sourceNote: Microsoft sources list March 14, 2002 for Europe.

items.title: Final Fantasy X
items.body: For many UK players, this is the first PS2 Final Fantasy that makes the new machine feel necessary.
items.sourceNote: PAL timing is spring 2002; verify exact UK release date before final copy.

items.title: The Elder Scrolls III: Morrowind
items.body: Bethesda makes a vast, alien RPG that feels like a world found inside a manual.
items.sourceNote: Initial PC release is widely placed in May 2002; Xbox timing follows soon after.

items.title: Warcraft III: Reign of Chaos
items.body: Blizzard turns RTS campaigns into character drama and Battle.net routine.
items.sourceNote: Commonly listed as July 2002.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: Xbox launches in Europe
items.body: The UK gets Microsoft's heavy black console in March, followed quickly by a painful price cut.
items.sourceNote: Microsoft and Guardian sources cover launch and price-cut context.

items.title: GameCube launches in Europe
items.body: Nintendo arrives with a lower UK price, tiny discs and a design that looks almost defiantly unlike Xbox.
items.sourceNote: WorthPlaying reports the May 3 European launch and UK price context.

items.title: PlayStation 2 dominates the shelf
items.body: By 2002, PS2 feels less like new hardware and more like the centre of gravity.
items.sourceNote: Guardian UK market context notes PS2 strength in Britain.

items.title: Console online begins to formalise
items.body: Xbox Live's North American launch points toward the next shape of console play, while Europe mostly reads about it from the side.
items.sourceNote: 2002 platform references support the cautious regional framing.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Dial-up culture
items.body: Battlefield 1942 makes online PC play feel bigger, louder and more vehicle-shaped.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Servers and services
items.body: Xbox Live launches in North America, making console online play feel like a service rather than an experiment.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Forums and files
items.body: UK players still live between dial-up reality, university broadband envy and LAN-party practicality.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Console online experiments
items.body: Cheat sites, walkthroughs and forums become part of how players survive Morrowind, Vice City, Kingdom Hearts and Metroid rumours.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like the console war had become physical: PS2 slim cases, Xbox bulk, GameCube handles and different demo discs competing for the same Saturday.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: Vice City made games feel louder outside gaming spaces, as if radio stations, clothes, cars and quotes had escaped the disc.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: The Xbox was impressive but had to explain itself. The GameCube was charming but had to prove it. The PS2 simply kept receiving games.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: PC players lived in another weather system: Morrowind maps, Warcraft III ladders, Battlefield servers and the sense that online play was becoming ordinary very slowly.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
